questions.question: Article 34.1 of the Constitution provides that 'Justice shall be administered in courts established by law by judges appointed in the manner provided by this Constitution, and, save in such special and limited cases as may be prescribed by law, shall be administered in public.' Which of the following most accurately identifies the three distinct requirements embedded in this provision?

options.A: Justice must be administered: (1) in courts; (2) by legally qualified persons; (3) in accordance with the rules of evidence
options.B: Justice must be administered: (1) in courts established by law; (2) by judges appointed in the constitutional manner; (3) in public, subject to limited exceptions prescribed by law
options.C: Justice must be administered: (1) in the High Court or superior courts; (2) by qualified judges; (3) in adversarial proceedings with representation for all parties
options.D: Justice must be administered: (1) in accordance with Article 40.3 (personal rights); (2) by judges with security of tenure; (3) within the territorial jurisdiction of the State

questions.explanation: Article 34.1 contains three distinct constitutional requirements that together define the judicial power: (1) justice must be administered in courts established by law — not by administrative bodies, ministerial tribunals, or ad hoc arrangements unless they meet the Article 37 limited functions test; (2) those courts must be presided over by judges appointed in the constitutional manner (Presidential appointment on Government advice, or as the Constitution prescribes); and (3) justice must be administered in public, with exceptions only for 'special and limited cases' prescribed by law (e.g., in camera proceedings in family law, criminal cases involving child victims). Each of these requirements is justiciable: a court-like body that fails any of these criteria may be found to be exercising judicial power unconstitutionally.

questions.question: Article 37 of the Constitution permits 'limited functions and powers of a judicial nature' to be exercised by bodies that are not courts under Article 34. Which of the following most precisely states the conditions under which Article 37 authorises such non-court exercise of judicial-type functions?

options.A: Any administrative body may exercise judicial-type functions provided it has a statutory basis and operates in accordance with natural justice, without any limit on the scope of the functions it may exercise
options.B: Article 37 permits bodies other than courts to exercise functions 'limited' in nature: the function must not involve a final and conclusive determination of legal rights in major and important matters; if it does, it must be reserved to courts
options.C: Article 37 permits any body chaired by a qualified lawyer to exercise judicial-type functions; the presence of a lawyer ensures the constitutional integrity of the process
options.D: Article 37 permits non-courts to exercise judicial functions only in civil matters; all criminal matters, regardless of their significance, must be adjudicated by a court established under Article 34

questions.explanation: Article 37 provides a critical exception to Article 34.1's exclusive vesting of judicial power in the courts: it permits non-court bodies to exercise 'limited functions and powers of a judicial nature in matters other than criminal matters.' The key word is 'limited': as elaborated in McDonald v Bord na gCon [1965] IR 217, if the function involves a final and conclusive determination of the major legal rights of citizens — particularly in significant matters — it falls outside what Article 37 permits and must be exercised by a court. The test is whether the function is truly 'limited' in scope and impact, or whether it is functionally equivalent to full judicial adjudication. Administrative tribunals (Competition and Consumer Protection Commission, Labour Court, Workplace Relations Commission) can operate under Article 37 if their determinations are limited and subject to court oversight.

questions.question: Article 35.4 of the Constitution provides the mechanism for the removal of a judge of a superior court. Which of the following correctly states that mechanism and the grounds on which removal is possible?

options.A: A superior court judge may be removed by the Government (Cabinet) for stated misbehaviour or incapacity after a report by the Chief Justice recommending removal
options.B: A superior court judge may be removed only by the President, following addresses from both Houses of the Oireachtas for stated misbehaviour or incapacity
options.C: A superior court judge may be removed by a two-thirds majority vote of Dail Eireann alone, for stated misbehaviour, incapacity, or where the judge has been convicted of a criminal offence
options.D: A superior court judge may be removed by the Supreme Court on an application by the Bar Council or Law Society following a finding of professional misconduct

questions.explanation: Article 35.4.1 degrees establishes the only constitutional mechanism for removing a superior court judge: the judge may be removed from office for stated misbehaviour or incapacity by the President, but only following resolutions calling for removal passed by both Houses of the Oireachtas. This 'address procedure' ensures that judicial removal is a parliamentary — not executive — act, reinforcing judicial independence. The requirement for both Houses means the Government alone cannot remove a judge (it would need at least Seanad agreement). In practice, this mechanism has never been invoked to completion in the history of the State, reflecting the constitutional entrenchment of judicial independence. The Judicial Council Act 2019 established a procedure for investigation of judicial conduct, but actual removal still requires the Article 35.4 mechanism.

questions.question: Article 35.5 of the Constitution provides that the remuneration of a judge shall not be reduced during the judge's continuance in office. In Redmond v Minister for the Environment [2001] 4 IR 61, the Supreme Court considered whether a general pay cut imposed on all public servants, including judges, violated this provision. Which of the following most accurately states the court's reasoning?

options.A: The court held that any reduction in judicial pay, however general in application, was an absolute constitutional violation of Article 35.5 and struck down the reduction as unconstitutional
options.B: The court held that because the pay reduction applied universally to all public servants and was not targeted at judges, it did not violate Article 35.5, which only prohibits reductions specifically directed at the judiciary
options.C: The court referred the question to the Council of State for an advisory opinion before determining the constitutional validity of the pay reduction measure
options.D: The court held that while Article 35.5 was absolute in its terms, emergency legislation could temporarily suspend its application during a period of national financial crisis

questions.explanation: The constitutional protection in Article 35.5 (salary not reduced during continuance in office) is designed to protect judicial independence by insulating judges from financial pressure applied by the government. However, in Redmond v Minister for the Environment [2001], and subsequently in matters arising from the Financial Emergency Measures in the Public Interest Acts 2009-2013, the courts held that general pay reductions applicable to all public servants (not targeted specifically at judges) do not violate Article 35.5. The rationale is that Art. 35.5 is a protection against politically motivated salary cuts directed at the judiciary, not a guarantee against general economic measures uniformly applied across the public sector. Note: a specific constitutional amendment (Thirtieth Amendment 2011) was subsequently passed to put this interpretation beyond doubt during the financial crisis.

questions.question: Article 34.3.2 degrees of the Constitution provides that a decision of the Supreme Court on a Bill referred under Article 26 shall be binding on all courts in respect of those provisions. Which of the following correctly identifies the most significant constitutional consequence of an Article 26 reference?

options.A: A Bill upheld by the Supreme Court in an Article 26 reference becomes immune from any future constitutional challenge, creating an irrebuttable presumption of constitutionality for the provisions referred
options.B: A Bill found repugnant in an Article 26 reference is automatically referred to a constitutional convention for revision before being re-presented to the Oireachtas
options.C: A Bill upheld in an Article 26 reference may subsequently be challenged on the same constitutional grounds by a differently constituted Supreme Court if new constitutional arguments emerge
options.D: A Bill partially found repugnant in an Article 26 reference — where only some provisions are referred — may subsequently be challenged on its unreferred provisions in an ordinary action

questions.explanation: The most significant consequence of a successful (i.e., constitutionality-upholding) Article 26 reference is the creation of an irrebuttable presumption of constitutionality for the provisions referred: Art. 34.3.2 degrees provides that a decision of the Supreme Court shall be 'binding on all courts.' This means that no future court may entertain a challenge to those specific provisions on constitutional grounds. This is a permanent and absolute bar — it cannot be overcome by new arguments, different plaintiffs, or changed circumstances. If a Bill is referred and found constitutional, it cannot subsequently be attacked. Conversely, if the President declines to refer a Bill, or refers only some provisions, the unreferred provisions retain only the ordinary rebuttable presumption of constitutionality and may be challenged in subsequent litigation (as happened with several provisions of Criminal Justice Acts).

questions.question: Article 34.4.6 degrees (as amended by the Thirty-Third Amendment 2013) provides that 'The decision of the Supreme Court shall in all cases be final and conclusive.' In what circumstances has the Supreme Court itself acknowledged limitations on this principle?

options.A: The principle is absolute; the Supreme Court has never acknowledged any limitation on the finality of its decisions and no appeal mechanism exists from Supreme Court decisions under any provision of the Constitution
options.B: The principle applies only to constitutional decisions; the Supreme Court has acknowledged that EU law creates an obligation to refer questions of EU law to the Court of Justice of the EU, which can effectively review the substance of Supreme Court decisions
options.C: The principle applies to domestic law; the Supreme Court can be overruled by the European Court of Human Rights under the European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003
options.D: The principle applies to all matters except referendums; the Supreme Court's decisions on referendum petitions can be reviewed by the President of the High Court in exceptional circumstances

questions.explanation: Article 34.4.6 degrees establishes the finality of Supreme Court decisions as a matter of Irish constitutional law. However, the Supreme Court has acknowledged that EU law creates an important practical qualification: under Article 267 TFEU (formerly Article 234 TEC), national courts of last instance have an obligation to refer questions of EU law to the Court of Justice of the EU for a preliminary ruling. Since the CJEU's interpretation of EU law is authoritative and binding on all national courts, a CJEU ruling can in practice require the Irish courts to apply EU law differently from how the Supreme Court had construed it. This reflects the primacy of EU law established in Costa v ENEL [1964] and Ireland's constitutional commitment to EU membership under Article 29.4. The ECHR (Option C) is incorporated via the 2003 Act as a statutory, not constitutional, instrument — the Supreme Court is not constitutionally bound by ECHR decisions.

questions.question: The Thirty-Third Amendment of the Constitution Act 2013 established the Court of Appeal as a new constitutional court between the High Court and the Supreme Court. Which of the following most accurately describes the constitutional effect of this amendment on the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction?

options.A: The amendment removed the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction entirely; the Court of Appeal now hears all appeals from the High Court as a matter of right, with no further appeal to the Supreme Court
options.B: The amendment established the Court of Appeal as the primary appellate court from the High Court; appeals to the Supreme Court are now limited to cases involving matters of general public importance or where the interests of justice require it (a leave requirement)
options.C: The amendment established the Court of Appeal to hear appeals in criminal matters only; civil appeals continue to lie from the High Court directly to the Supreme Court as of right
options.D: The amendment is purely administrative and makes no change to the Supreme Court's constitutional appellate jurisdiction; the Court of Appeal operates as an additional tier for matters the Supreme Court declines to hear

questions.explanation: The Thirty-Third Amendment (2013) established the Court of Appeal and fundamentally restructured the appellate system. Under the new Article 34.5, the Court of Appeal hears most appeals from the High Court. Appeals to the Supreme Court from the Court of Appeal require leave and are limited to cases involving 'a matter of general public importance' or where 'the interests of justice require' the appeal (Art. 34.5.3 degrees). This replaced the previous system where appeals lay directly to the Supreme Court as of right from the High Court, which had created a significant backlog. The amendment also preserved the Supreme Court's power to hear 'leapfrog' appeals directly from the High Court in exceptional circumstances (Art. 34.5.4 degrees). The reform significantly limited the range of cases the Supreme Court hears, positioning it as a genuine apex court.

questions.question: Bula Ltd v Tara Mines Ltd [2000] 4 IR 412 is a leading Irish case on judicial independence and the principle against apparent bias. Which of the following most accurately states the test applied by the Supreme Court?

options.A: The court applied a purely subjective test: whether the judge in question personally believed they were biased against one of the parties
options.B: The court applied a two-stage test: first, whether there was actual proven bias; and second, only if actual bias could not be proved, whether a reasonable person might apprehend a risk of bias
options.C: The court applied the objective test of whether a reasonable, informed observer would apprehend a real danger of bias, taking into account all the circumstances of the case including the judge's connections to the parties
options.D: The court held that no judicial disqualification for apparent bias is possible in Ireland, as Article 35.2 (judicial oath) provides a conclusive guarantee of judicial impartiality

questions.explanation: In Bula Ltd v Tara Mines [2000] 4 IR 412, the Supreme Court adopted the objective test for apparent bias: whether a reasonable person, fully informed of all the relevant facts and circumstances, would apprehend a real danger or possibility of bias on the part of the judge. This replaced older tests based on 'reasonable suspicion' and aligns with Article 6(1) ECHR (right to an independent and impartial tribunal). The test requires more than mere speculation or hypersensitive concern — there must be an objective basis for the apprehension. In Bula itself, the challenged judge had previously acted as a barrister for one of the parties, raising apparent bias concerns. The case established that judges with prior significant involvement in the same matter or related matters should generally recuse themselves.

questions.question: Article 35.3 of the Constitution prohibits judges from being members of the Oireachtas or holding 'any other office or position of emolument'. A judge is offered a part-time role as an independent statutory arbitrator, paid on a per-case basis. Which of the following most accurately states the constitutional position?

options.A: The appointment is permissible; Article 35.3 only prohibits judges from holding positions within the three branches of government, not independent statutory roles
options.B: The appointment is impermissible if it involves 'emolument' (financial remuneration); a part-time paid role as arbitrator would likely fall within the prohibition, compromising judicial independence and impartiality
options.C: The appointment is permissible with Government consent, as Article 35.3 applies only to full-time positions and the Government may waive the restriction for part-time roles
options.D: The appointment is permissible if approved by the Chief Justice, who has inherent constitutional power to determine the extent of the Article 35.3 prohibition for serving judges

questions.explanation: Article 35.3 provides that no judge shall engage in any other employment 'or hold any other office or position of emolument.' This prohibition is broad and deliberately so: it is designed to protect judicial independence (judges must not have financial interests that could create conflicts) and the integrity of the judicial office. A paid arbitration role — even part-time — would almost certainly constitute an 'office or position of emolument' within Article 35.3. The prohibition exists independently of judicial independence concerns: it is a bright-line constitutional rule. Some exceptions are constitutionally provided (e.g., a judge may be assigned to chair a commission or tribunal without emolument, as some judges serve on Law Reform Commission projects in an honorary capacity), but paid positions are prohibited.

questions.question: In O'Brien v Bord na Mona [1983] IR 255, the Supreme Court considered whether the Bord na Mona Act conferred impermissible judicial power on a non-judicial body. The court applied the McDonald v Bord na gCon test and upheld the statutory scheme. Which feature of the scheme led the court to conclude that Article 37 permitted it rather than requiring a court?

options.A: The functions were non-determinative — the body could only make recommendations that required ministerial ratification before taking legal effect, preserving the executive's ultimate control
options.B: The functions were 'limited' in nature — they involved the determination of compensation for compulsory acquisition (a defined and bounded issue) rather than a wide-ranging adjudication of general legal rights and liabilities
options.C: The functions were purely administrative — they involved assessing technical matters of peat bog valuation, which are by their nature incapable of constituting the administration of justice
options.D: The functions were subject to full de novo appeal to the courts on both fact and law, making the initial determination merely provisional and therefore not constituting the final administration of justice

questions.explanation: In O'Brien v Bord na Mona [1983] IR 255, the Supreme Court upheld the statutory scheme for compulsory acquisition and compensation determination by Bord na Mona under Article 37. The key was that the functions were genuinely 'limited' in the McDonald sense: the body determined a specific, bounded question (compensation for the compulsory acquisition of peat bogs) rather than adjudicating general legal rights. The Article 37 permission is tied to the 'limited' nature of the functions — compensation bodies, planning appeal boards, and similar bodies with defined and circumscribed jurisdictions generally fall within the Article 37 exception. However, where a body's jurisdiction expands to encompass wide-ranging determinations of legal rights and liabilities — approaching the comprehensive adjudicative function of a court — it crosses the line and requires court status.

questions.question: Article 34.1 requires that justice be administered 'in public', subject to limited statutory exceptions. The Supreme Court in In the Matter of a Ward of Court [1995] 2 ILRM 401 considered the scope of the in camera exception in family/medical proceedings. Which of the following most accurately describes the constitutional approach to in camera orders?

options.A: In camera orders are constitutionally impermissible; Article 34.1's requirement of public justice is absolute and admits of no exceptions regardless of the sensitivity of the subject matter
options.B: In camera orders may be made by a court at common law without any statutory basis, provided the court determines that the interests of justice in a particular case require privacy
options.C: In camera orders require a statutory basis ('prescribed by law' under Article 34.1); the court's inherent power to sit in private cannot substitute for the legislative authorisation that the Constitution requires
options.D: In camera orders are automatically available in all family law and medical cases without any statutory requirement, on the basis that the right to privacy under Article 40.3 implicitly overrides Article 34.1's in public requirement

questions.explanation: Article 34.1's phrase 'save in such special and limited cases as may be prescribed by law' establishes that exceptions to the in public requirement must be grounded in legislation: the Oireachtas must authorise in camera proceedings. Courts cannot create exceptions to the public hearing requirement purely through their inherent jurisdiction — the constitutional text requires that exceptions be 'prescribed by law.' In Ireland, the Courts Act 1971, the Courts Act 1981, and various Family Law Acts provide the statutory basis for in camera family law hearings. The 'special and limited' qualification also means that in camera orders should be genuinely exceptional and confined to the categories prescribed — not a general discretion to exclude the public whenever convenient.

questions.question: The Workplace Relations Commission (WRC) adjudicates on employment law disputes, with a right of appeal to the Labour Court and then to the High Court on a point of law. A litigant argues that the WRC is exercising judicial power unconstitutionally because it makes binding determinations of rights. Which of the following provides the strongest constitutional analysis supporting the WRC's validity under the Constitution?

options.A: The WRC is unconstitutional because it makes binding determinations of legal rights — an activity that can only be carried out by a court established under Article 34
options.B: The WRC is constitutional under Article 37 because its functions are limited in nature (employment-specific disputes), it is established by statute, and full de novo appeal to the courts preserves ultimate judicial supervision of legal rights
options.C: The WRC is constitutional because employment law matters are executive functions within the scope of Article 28, not judicial functions within Article 34
options.D: The WRC is constitutional because the Oireachtas has the power under Article 15.2 to vest any function, including judicial functions, in any body it creates

questions.explanation: The WRC's constitutional validity rests on Article 37: it exercises 'limited functions and powers of a judicial nature in matters other than criminal matters.' The functions are limited — they are confined to defined statutory employment rights disputes (unfair dismissal, redundancy, discrimination, etc.) rather than general civil jurisdiction. The statutory basis (Workplace Relations Act 2015) satisfies the 'established by law' requirement. Critically, the availability of full appeal to the Labour Court and onward appeal to the High Court on a point of law ensures that ultimate judicial supervision of legal rights is preserved — the WRC's determination is not necessarily final, maintaining access to Article 34 courts. Option D is incorrect: Article 15.2 gives the Oireachtas legislative power, not the power to redistribute judicial power to non-courts beyond what Art. 37 permits.

questions.question: Article 36 of the Constitution empowers the Oireachtas to regulate the constitution, powers, jurisdiction and procedure of the courts 'as shall be prescribed by law.' Which of the following most accurately describes the limit on this legislative power to regulate the courts?

options.A: Article 36 gives the Oireachtas plenary power to reorganise the courts in any manner it chooses, including abolishing individual courts or transferring their jurisdiction to administrative bodies
options.B: Article 36 allows the Oireachtas to regulate procedural and structural matters but cannot be used to abolish the constitutional courts (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, High Court) or strip them of their essential constitutional jurisdiction
options.C: Article 36 is limited to procedural matters; any structural change to the courts system (creation of new courts, abolition of existing courts) requires a constitutional amendment
options.D: Article 36 is purely declaratory; the courts derive their authority from Articles 34 to 37 alone and the Oireachtas has no power to alter judicial structures established by those Articles

questions.explanation: Article 36 grants the Oireachtas regulatory power over the courts — but this is not plenary. The Oireachtas can prescribe by law the constitution, powers, jurisdiction and procedure of the courts (e.g., establishing circuit court areas, regulating court fees, prescribing procedural rules). However, the Constitution itself establishes the superior courts (Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, High Court) and their essential constitutional functions — the Oireachtas cannot abolish these courts or strip them of their core constitutional jurisdiction, as that would involve amending the Constitution without referendum. The Oireachtas can create inferior courts (Circuit Court, District Court, Special Criminal Court) by statute and can reform their structure and jurisdiction, but the essential constitutional court architecture protected by Articles 34-37 is beyond ordinary legislative amendment.

questions.question: Kerins v McGuinness [2019] IESC 11 is a significant recent Supreme Court case concerning the constitutional rights of a citizen called before an Oireachtas committee. How does this case develop the principle established in Maguire v Ardagh (Abbeylara) [2002] in relation to Oireachtas committee proceedings?

options.A: Kerins overruled Maguire v Ardagh entirely, holding that Oireachtas committees may make findings of individual culpability provided they adopt adversarial procedures equivalent to court proceedings
options.B: Kerins confirmed and extended Maguire v Ardagh: where an Oireachtas committee's proceedings could have a significant adverse effect on a named individual's constitutional rights (reputation, livelihood), the individual is entitled to fair procedures, including the right to cross-examine and to be represented, even if the committee is not making a formal finding of culpability
options.C: Kerins held that Oireachtas committee proceedings are entirely non-justiciable; any challenge to the procedures adopted by a committee must be addressed through the political process rather than the courts
options.D: Kerins held that only members of the Oireachtas have constitutional rights when appearing before committees; public servants and private citizens testifying before committees have no constitutional entitlement to fair procedures

questions.explanation: Kerins v McGuinness [2019] IESC 11 arose from Angela Kerins's appearances before the Oireachtas Public Accounts Committee and the devastation to her reputation and health caused by the proceedings. The Supreme Court confirmed the Maguire v Ardagh principle (committees cannot make judicial-type findings of culpability) and significantly extended its application: even where a committee stops short of formal culpability findings, if the proceedings have a seriously adverse effect on a named citizen's constitutional rights — particularly their good name (Art. 40.3.2 degrees) and livelihood — that citizen is entitled to fair procedures, including the right to know the case against them, to cross-examine, and to be represented. The case is significant as it imposed constitutional constraints on how Oireachtas committees must conduct investigations involving named private citizens.
